sentences.text: First of all, his name was Hans Biebow.
Whether he was a Gestapo man, I don't-- I personally don't know.
He was not in uniform.
No, he was never in uniform.
I never saw him.
I mean I never saw him personally.
But as far as I know, he was not in uniform.
Also as far as I know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he supervised all the work that was being done or that was done in the ghetto.
He was in charge-- he was in complete charge of the ghetto.
So he gave orders to the Judenrat.
He followed-- he gave orders to the Judenrat.
And I, I understand that he got orders from the higher authorities or from the military authorities to be carried out in the Lodz ghetto.
Yes.
Right.
Well, I worked until May 1 of 1945, the same type of work.
We got up in the morning like at 5 o'clock.
We had the roll call till about 6:00.
And we were marched about 5 kilometers to the place of work.
And we worked there till 5 o'clock in the afternoon. 12 o'clock, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] noon, we received a little bit of soup, a watery soup.
If somebody was lucky, he will find a piece of leaf or whatever in it.
In the evening we came home, back to the camp.
Again, we were counted, at the roll-call place.
And we got another soup and a piece of bread.
And we went up to our bunks, and we lay down.
We had the soup, and we rested.
And we went to sleep.
And this was day after day after day.
On May 1, we came home-- 'home', I call it 'home.' We came back to camp for work.
And we got our daily ration, just like any other day.
And the loud-- on the loudspeaker it was announced that we are no longer going out to work, because a typhus epidemic broke out in the camp.
And we will be isolated.
No one is allowed to leave the premises of the building.
When I say 'the premises of the building'-- because people would roam around the roll-call place, let's say on a Sunday, when-- incidentally, we worked six days a week, except for Sunday.
And on Sunday, whoever was capable of walking around, he would come down from the bunks and walk around on that roll-call place.
We were not allowed to do it, because of this epidemic.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Literally, we did not receive this little soup what we got previously.
People were dying by the hundreds, every single day.
People were going down from the bunk-- either to the latrine or wherever-- and they dropped dead on the floor, and they lay there.
And those who would go down and-- they would trip over them.
Those who were still-- were able to walk, they would trip over them, over the dead bodies.
And they would continue walking.
Or they would fall over them.

The amount of death was so high, and kept on rising, that the group of people who were-- who were supposed to take care of them, take out the bodies and wheel them over to this ditch and drop them there-- couldn't keep up with the amount of these dead bodies.
And the bodies were piling up.
And this is the condition that we found, when we came on May 1.
And these bodies were mounting and mounting.
Until the night of May the 7th, when we heard dull cannon sounds of explosions.
And we realized that something is-- something is going on.
We literally didn't dare to hope that this was the end.
Because the thought of the war to end was so remote for us.
We just couldn't believe that something like this could ever happen.
Every one of us were resigned that I will be the next one to drop dead on the ground or on the bunk that I was laying.
But nevertheless, about-- after midnight, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] inmates came running in, into the building, yelling that the gates are open, and the Germans left.
Now, in order to elaborate on it, I have to say our camp consisted of three-story-- of a three-story wooden building.
The barrack was a big, three-story wooden building.
I was on the second or the third floor, I don't even remember anymore.
But on the ground floor were those who were still capable of walking around and so on.
Adjacent to our building was a brick building, where the German SS lived and slept.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And about midnight, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] May the 8th, those were walking around, from the ground floor, noticed that the gates from the camp were open.
And the doors of this adjacent building, of this brick building, were open, and the Germans were running out.
So they realized that something is happening.
And they came running, whoever was able-- capable of doing-- to tell us the good news that the Germans left.
We were not capable of doing anything about it.
I understand-- I heard later on-- that some of those who were capable of walking would go out of the camp, in the middle of the night.
And they would move out to, to, to the adjacent farms, to go into the-- to the houses of the-- of the farmers.
As a matter of fact, a friend of mine who lives here, not far, was one of them who came into one of those farmers.
And he stayed with them for a while.
German farmers.
Because it was German territory.
I really don't know.
But it must have been close to 1,000.
Yes.
Close to 1,000.
Because it was a huge building-- three floors.
And it was packed, packed with people.
And the floors were, were full of, full of bodies.
Maybe, maybe 200 or even less than that.
For eight days.
--For eight days.
And, in this time period, several hundred--
Died.
Yes.
We didn't receive any medical treatment for the-- since 1940.
And the ghetto-- you see, we had, we had hospitals in the ghetto.
We had doctors in the ghetto.
But the, the, the medication was so scarce that the normal inhabitant of the ghetto couldn't get to it.
Because, in order to get medication, you-- you have to have, like, influence.
You had to know somebody, in order to get it.
In the normal-- so-called normal times-- I would call the ghetto 'normal times' only to a-- to an extent.
Because we were together with the family, and we were together in our own home, as opposed to a, to a camp.
But as far as the home itself is concerned, we had no heating, we had no hot water or-- in many-- in, in a majority of homes, we didn't have any regular cold water.
We had to bring it from, from other places.
This was the normal times of the ghetto.
I would say that, after the second-- I would say, after, after the second winter in the ghetto, you wouldn't find one piece of wooden furniture standing in good order.
Because everything was chopped up, in order to heat the rooms in the winter.
Forget about coal or, or wood to, to, to warm the houses.
We didn't have it.
Now, coming back to May 8th, after the Germans left, about 10 o'clock in the morning, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a civilian Russian car drove into the camp.
The Germans, of course, were gone.
The gates were open.
And two German-- I'm sorry, two Russian officers jump out of the car, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with machine guns in their hands, and looking around, not realizing what it is.
And then they saw the faces, staring out from the paneless windows.
Because the windows-- the windows in that barrack that we were we didn't have any, any, any glass, just open, open holes.
And they saw these faces.
They realized what it was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They called us out.
So those who were capable of walking out came out.
And they surrounded this car.
One of the officers got on the roof of the car.
And he spoke to us, naturally, in Russian.
And, since some of the inmates knew Russian, or at least part of it-- Because, don't forget, there were elderly Jews, who still remembered the Russians from the, the First World War.
And they lived under the Russian-- under the Russian occupation under the Czar.
And they knew a certain amount of Russian.
And when this Russian soldier, the officer, spoke to us, he could more or less translate-- maybe not word for word, but at least more or less the general meaning of what he said.
All I remember was that the Russian officer told us that the Russian army came in from, from deep Russia.
They are now on the German territory.
And they are here, and we are free.
We should remain calm.
We should remain in places where we are-- that where we were-- not that we were able of doing anything or going someplace.
And the Russian army will come in soon.
Of course, we all started to cry.
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] Just a minute.
No one-- no one of us believed that-- [SIGH] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that day will ever come.
No, we, we realized that it is true.
But no one believed that, that we will live to see this day.
Let's put it this way.
And they left.

And about two hours later, the Russian army moved through the camp-- not into the camp, but whoever was able, capable, of walking out, they walked out from the camp on the road, to greet the Russians.
And they moved, moved around-- you know, they moved by.
And they started to throw food at us, everything that they had.
And we started to accumulate food.
I don't know how and from where, I happened to manage to found a straw sack.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some, some inmates had, like a, like a sack made of, of, from, from straw, like-- from a straw material.
And it was filled with straw for their heads.
So I would take this, shake out the straw, and I would fill up this sack with any kind of food that I can manage to get from these Russian soldiers.
So I had a piece of cheese and cans, cans of food and pieces of butter, bread, and you name it.
And I packed it up, everything, in that bag.
And I held it-- onto dear life.
Towards the evening-- and the Russian army went, went out of this area.
Towards the evening, a group of German women and German old men were-- came into our camp.
I wouldn't say that they were chased by the Russian soldiers.
But they were guarded by Russian soldiers and officers.
And they came into the camp.
And they were ordered-- first of all, the man went in-- the Russian-- the Germans went in to the Russian-- to the German building and clean up the building.
Where the SS guards were.
They also brought in bundles of linens and clothing for us.
The German woman put together some sort of a bath, to bathe us.
A bath.
And we took off our-- they took off the clothes from us, and they gave us a bath.
And they gave us some sort of, like, a light shirt or a pajamas or top of, top of pajamas or the lower part of pajamas, just, just to cover our bodies with something.
And two German women would take us under the arm.
Because I'm, I'm speaking to what they did to me, and they did naturally to everybody else of us.
And they brought us in, into the building that was previously occupied by the German SS.
And they put us in a real bed.
Because it was where the Germans slept before, with bedding and white linen and everything else.
And we didn't see this-- not only not in the camp but even in the ghetto we didn't have these things anymore.
And it was something, the biggest luxury that anybody can experience.
Something you hadn't seen in five years.
In five years, exactly.
We couldn't believe it.
Yeah, because, you know-- to help us walk.
Because we were all so, so weak.
And they put us down there.
Some of the Russian officers took off their uniforms.
They put on white uniforms, because they were doctors.
And they took away all the food that we accumulated during the day.
What we carried on, the way we fought the German women and men who took away the food from us, it was indescribable.
Because I didn't see-- I didn't see so much food that I had in this bag in the last five years.

And I didn't dare-- I sat down after the, after the Russian army left our area, and before the evening came, late in the afternoon, when the Germans came-- when the German women and men came in to take care of us, under the supervision of the, of the Russians, I was sitting on the ground, with this bag, hold-- you know, I was holding on to dear life.
And I didn't dare to eat much.
Because I was afraid I wouldn't have enough for tomorrow.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I kept on eating, little by little-- you know, snack by snack, hoping that I'll have-- you  know, it's-- in my, in my, in my subconscious, I was still living with the thought about to have food for the next day.
And here they come in, and, and they, they were trying, first, to take away the food from me.
And they were trying to explain to me, and pleading with me, I shouldn't worry that I will have food from now on.
I wouldn't believe them.
Naturally, who would?
Why do you think they took the food away?
Because it was-- they were afraid-- because it was contaminated.
Because it-- I was holding on.
And I was as filthy as anything, with all kinds of germs and everything else.
So they took away everything from us-- the clothing and everything else, and naturally the food, too.
They destroyed it.
Little did I know that, when I finally was put in bed, in the, in the place where the Germans used to live, they started to cook the food for us.
And they gave us-- the following morning, they, they woke us up, 5 o'clock in the morning.
They gave us-- I don't remember exactly the kind of food that we got.
But they fed us.
They wouldn't allow-- they wouldn't allow us to eat by ourselves.
They fed us every two hours a little bit, throughout the whole day, little by little, little by little.
Because our, our system, our condition, our bodies, were in such a condition that we couldn't-- they, they wouldn't let us eat too much at one time.
And many, many-- I don't know the amount of, of us-- died, still died after that.
That was too late for them to observe or to come back to themselves.
But they prevented anybody from killing themself by eating too fast--
Oh, yes, because a lot-- a lot-- people, people died, I would say, because they ate so much the very first day when they got the food from the Russian soldiers.
I myself, I myself-- you see, if you open up a can of, let's say, conserved meat, and you ate it together with butter-- because everything, everything was, was not enough for us.
So we grabbed everything that, that we could at one time.
And whoever was conscious enough-- and, and, you know-- to understand in the condition that he is, that to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] discipline himself not to eat, maybe this helped him to survive.
And others just couldn't help themselves, which is understandable.
Yes.
--Into some kind of a field hospital.
That's right.
We were, we were in this place a couple of weeks, maybe five, six weeks.
And then we were sent out into town, into town.
There was a, a, a children's home for-- a [INAUDIBLE].
It was a German, a German, little town.
In Dornhau.
There was a, a-- it used to be a German children's home.
And it was converted for us.
Something like this, yes.
I-- you see--
After all, you know, it's about 50 years ago.
And it is difficult to remember the exact dates.
I can only-- I can only remember the, the things that happened.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
It was summer.
Because I remember, we were capable of walking around more or less normally.
And we got different clothes, once we were there, we were sent out to this, to this home.
Yes?
I, I really don't-- I really don't remember the amount of us.
Well it must have been at least 150, maybe 200.
And we were walking around-- this I remember that was, because it was nice and warm.
So it must have been either June, July, or something.
Because we were walking around on the grounds.
They were beautiful, beautiful kept grounds.
And we walked around.
And, and then we would come back to the-- you know, inside.
We had regular dining room, and so on.
Yes.
By the Russians.
That's right.
Especially-- and it was especially for us.
And then, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if I'm not mistaken, some other inmates from other camps came into the same home.
Because, I remember, there was a doctor that-- we never knew him-- came in, a Jewish doctor, a former inmate came in.
And he was in charge of this home.
And he took in some German women, they used to be nurses.
And there were nurses in this home for us.
Oh, yes.
We were all still recuperating.
We were not allowed to leave the premises of this camp-- of this, of this home.
The home-- it was building, a beautiful building, surrounded, surrounded by a-- not a fence, per se, but like you'd see fences here.
A nice, you know--
Like a-- no, it was not a picket fence.
It was made either of brick or something, but it was colorful.
A wall, like, yes.
And it was-- the grounds were immaculately kept, nice and clean.
And we would roam around this area.
But we were not allowed to go out yet into the streets.
It was not in quarantine.
But we were still considered not well enough to mingle with the, with the general population.
But later on, eventually, we did, we did go out.
And we started to get, to get in contact with inmates that were liberated from different camps.
They came to us.
And, little by little, we went to their camps, using all kind of transportation that we could find.
And this is how we started to get together, and we started to talk and to hear things.
Some of them went back to, to their-- to our home towns or cities, to look for relatives or family members.
And then we started to hear things that no one, no one is alive and so on.
So most of us decided to stay where we-- where we were and not to go back to Lodz.
I never, I never went back to Lodz.
